en: Next into the tank is a business that aspires to make
unmanned flight accessible for everyone.
Hi, I'm J.D. Claridge. And I'm Charles Manning.
Our company is X-Craft. We're looking to
seek $500,000 in exchange for 20%
of the company. Much like when the Wright brothers first took flight
in 1903, we're witnessing a new dawn of aviation.
The age of unmanned flight. The X-Plus-1 is a drone
redefined. We've combined both the hovering precision
of a helicopter and the fast flight of an aircraft.
You can soar at speeds of up to 60 miles an hour and climb
as high as 10,000 feet. And you can seamlessly transition from high-speed
back to hover. How cool. X-Craft is breaking boundaries
and with your help, the sky will no longer be the limit.
J.D., how is it different from all the traditional
drones I can buy today? So the fundamental difference is the ability to transition to forward flight.
And it does that with a wing. This is the wing right here. This
main area is the wing. Okay. So it's hovering and then when you want to go into forward flight,
it rotates like that. Oh, I see. Now it's an airplane.
Why do I care the fact that I can go so much faster?
You can capture a fast-moving subject like a car or
a motorcycle or something like that. And what's the range on this? Because
if it's going 60 miles an hour, you'd have to probably be in a helicopter
to accompany it, right? Or you can be stationary. You can be stationary, yeah.
I mean, definitely it's maneuverable so you can stay within visual
range. But one key part is that this system actually can run entirely autonomously.
You can build a flight plan on a software system on a PC and have it go
do those things and come back. Charles, am I allowed to create a flight
plan for an unmanned drone and just lay it out there? Yes, that's a good
question, Robert. There are regulations and rules right now. And
primarily, if you're flying for recreational purposes, you need to
keep it within line of sight, stay away from other aircraft and
airports, as well as personal property. And then you can't fly any
higher than 400 feet. How much does it cost you to make? It costs us
$400 to make it. And what do you sell it for? It retails right now
for $1,800. Have you sold any? Yeah, we've got $173,000 right now in
presales. And that's Kickstarter then? Yes, Kickstarter was $143,000
and then we've done about $30,000 on our website. Guys, what's your
background? Are you engineers or? Yeah, so I have worked, I'm an
aerospace engineer by trade. I started out of school and worked at
Oracle and have been in software my whole history. I was looking at
some drones yesterday, actually, because I have a property with 36
acres and I want to buy something because I'm getting a designation
of managed forest. And one of my responsibilities is to scan it every
year to look for diseased trees. So I'm going to buy a drone. Can I
configure the camera on a gyroscope below it so that I can film from
below? Because I have to turn it into airplane mode to use that
camera the way you've got it configured. We're working on it right
now so that we can actually hover it, invert it. And... Oh, there's
more. Yeah. So this is called Phone Drone. A what? Phone Drone.
Phone Drone. Bingo. So we can use our smartphones for all different
sorts of things today. That's great. But never have we been able to
allow our smartphones to fly. Until now. I love this stuff. And we
have a little video that we can show you. Sure, we'd love to see
that. Oh, my God. Oh, that is so cool. And you use the sensors in
the phone to do the balance and everything? Exactly. We do. So,
yeah. Smartphones today have gyros and accelerometers. They've got
GPS. They've got... A lot of them have very decent cameras. So really
that's all the expensive components in a drone. So we've
basically turned a smartphone into a drone. How much does this one
cost to make and how much do you sell it for? This is not
currently for sale. This is in development. What do you think it
will cost? We're looking at around a price point retail of $300.
Probably less than $100 to me. JD answered this for me. Where's the
secret sauce in your company? Like, tell me what somebody can't
knock off. You know what it is? It's really about the IP behind the
designs. The IP behind this design is unique. The IP behind this
design is unique and also patent pending. What we believe is that
there is completely opportunity around the designs. We're going to
license this thing. Silly. Music to my ears. And do you have the
ability or the contacts to go out and license yourself? Oh, we do.
So then I'm trying to figure out why are you here? You're asking
for $500,000. What are you going to use that money for? $250,000 we
would like to use for scaling production on the X Plus One to get
our costs down. So inventory. And then the other $250,000 is to
finish development of Phone Drum. Okay, well, what value are you
looking for? I've heard enough. I'd like to make an offer. I'd like to
start things out here because I've been looking at this market quite a
bit for the last month. And I think you have some issues around
licensing I could really help you with. But I think you need more
money. I'm going to give you $750,000 for 25%. Kevin coming into the
Cuban zone. No royalty, no nothing. I was going to ask the same thing,
David. Turned over a new leaf today. And the Grinch's heart be two
sizes that day. So $750,000 for 25%. Wow, guys, he increased your value
to $3 million. I appreciate that. You know what I smell? I smell a
nasty, nasty shark fight that's about to happen. I'll offer you a
million dollars for 25%. I'll raise mine to a million as well. I'll give
you a million. All right. I'll do the same thing. A million for 20%.
Wait, slow down. Slow down. Hang on. Maybe we should go step out in the
hall. Yes, we should all go step out in the hall. Before you step out in
the hall, we contemplated this as an opportunity. And it's a fantastic
opportunity. We believe everything is about team. That's why we are a
good team. Let him talk. Let him talk. No, let him talk. So let me just
unpack this, Damien. No, I want to do a team deal because I know that
there's a big, nasty money bag vulture somewhere here being quiet. So
if you guys want to talk about doing... Let's hear what he's going to
say. Here's my suggestion. Is there a capacity to do a syndicate where we
get each and every one of you participating in your own unique ways?
Oh, I'm interested in that. I'm interested in that. I'm interested in
that. For what value? That's very rare in the Shark Tank. I need enough
equity to make a difference. Charles, what would you say that dollar is
for you and the equity? So I think a $10 million valuation. Whoa, whoa,
whoa, whoa. Guys, how did you go from $2.5 million to $10 million? I
smell greedy people now. You know, I used to tell my daughter this little
story. Why did the baby crab want to share his food? And the answer is
because he was just a little shelfish, right? I can't counter for everybody
here, but I don't think this is worth more than $6 million. I don't believe
it. So that's good feedback. He got a ceiling, and that's helpful. I think
that ceiling for me is 5%. I got to have 5%, minimum. That's the smallest
position I've got in anything that I'm working hard on. So let me ask you a
question. Are you not interested in participating? Because that will help
address that problem pretty quickly. I'm interested. I see a lot of oil and
gas, security, all kinds of applications. I'm interested. We're all
interested. Mark, are you interested? Well, I don't know. Mark's not saying
anything. He hasn't said. We'll see. I think it'd be good to chat. Let's step
out. Okay. All right. Thank you. Just want you guys to be clear. There's a
lot of privacy issues. This isn't a slam dunk. It's your call. I think we
should try to get them all. At a $6 million valuation for 25%, if there's
five of us, simple math, we each have to put up $300,000. Mark? I don't know.
What's your reservation, Mark? I don't know. I want to hear what they have to
say. All right. We're excited. We're mostly excited about doing something
together. Okay. Do you have something to propose to us? We do. Yes. We would
like to propose $1.5 million, each at 5% equity, and bring all the sharks in.
You're willing to give up 25% of the company. We each put up $300,000 for
5%, 25% total. That gives you a $6 million valuation. I'm okay with this
deal. I'm one shark. I would do that deal. I'm okay with that deal. I'm okay
with that deal as well. That's three. Okay. Robert? Then I'm in on that deal
too. Mark, what are you doing? You guys are smart. Did anybody else offer
you money prior to this? For sure. Okay. Yeah. And so when someone's come to
you, what were the discussions? There's really two categories of
conversations. One is strategic, and one is kind of pseudo-institutional.
A lot of the institutional guys want to kind of shape it like an early stage
VC structure, not something we're as excited about doing. You all have a lot
more operational execution experience, respectfully, than many traditional
early stage VC guys. And you also cover a spectrum. You cover a variety of
different areas, which is really useful to the business, because that's the whole
model. Charles, I think that's so smart. I deal with a lot of VC guys like we all
do, and I got to tell you, they're all super smart. They're all great theory,
but they don't get their hands dirty in running businesses. That makes a lot of sense. All right, Mark, are you in?
That works. All right. We're in. I'm really excited. Good job. We should do a
special party where we do a little flight party. Absolutely. We got a shark
tank syndicate. Can you believe it? I'm excited. You guys are going to own this
market. Thank you, guys. Thank you. We're thrilled. All right. Well done.
Hi. I'm Morgan. And I'm Arlie. And we're seeking $300,000 for 25% of our
company. And we're your shark tank weather girls. Today in the tank, it's
going to be 75 degrees and sunny, with a 20% chance of rain. Perfect day for
some shopping. Come on. Hurry up. Let's go. Oh, no. Emergency weather update.
It looks like Hurricane Barbara is about to hit the tank. Quick. Everybody,
take out your rain gear. Oh, no. It's really starting to come down. Morgan,
what about our handbags? They're going to get ruined. If only there was a
raincoat for your handbag. There is, silly. We invented it. We're the sisters
behind the handbag raincoat. The handbag raincoat is a chic and innovative way
to protect your handbag from all Mother Nature's elements. It comes in three
different sizes. We've got you covered, no matter how much you like to tow it
around. With the handbag raincoat, you never have to worry about getting stuck
in the rain again. So, sharks, who wants to join us and own the rain with the
handbag raincoat? And now you can have those as some samples. Are you okay?
Yes. A little wet, but we're okay. Just a little wet. So, Barbara, we have one
for you. Yellow, my favorite color. Thank you. Glory. Pink. Mr. Wonderful for your
wife. Thank you so much. How do you know I don't have a bag I want to cover?
Thank you. Thank you. Mark, this is for your wife, also. Thank you.
What do you sell them for, and what does it cost to make? It costs us $1.79
landed. All sizes are the same price? All sizes are the same. They wholesale for $5, $7, and $9,
and they retail for $11, $15, and $20. Can you tell us your background? So, first,
and I love fashion. I majored in fashion, and I worked for six and a half years as the director
of merchandising for a large handbag company with 80 girls. And every day it was raining at work,
all the girls would be like, I'm not using my handbag today, or I'm going to use the worst
handbag in my closet. I call Arlie. I'm like, we need to figure out protection. So, we bought
shower curtains. We started cutting them on our living room floor with scissors, and we came up
with our patent, utility patent pending for the closure for the hook and loop closure of the
Velcro. It lets you get in your bag easily, and then seal it back up. This is a new category.
This never existed before. What have you experienced in just sales? And in what period
of time? How long have you been in business? We've been in business just about three years
right now. And to date, we've done over $600,000 in sales. Do you guys always talk in stereo at
home? We live only 22 months apart, so it's kind of... Is there a mind meld where you actually
talk together? I'm super cute. You're almost like twins. What about this year? That's just inception.
What about this year? This year, we've done $125,000 in sales. You sell entirely online,
or you're also selling retail? No. First, we did the test market online at Bed Bath & Beyond.
We passed that. Then they put us in 20 doors, then 100 doors. And they reordered. And they
just got into Nordstrom Bed Bath Container Store. Why have your sales not blown up? $125,000 for
a great idea like this should be in the millions. And I don't understand... So we have not done
marketing or advertising yet. We feel that we've done these sales organically. We were actually in
Oprah's Magazine. From there, we were put on The View. We sold 10,000 units. So right now,
we're at a break-even. How much money have you put in? We raised $360,000. Whoa. Through Friends
and Family. And it was basically to help us get off the ground to buy our first few rounds of
inventory. Did you sell them any of the company for that? So we own around 84% of the company.
Don't you think people are going to knock this off pretty fast? So we do have two design patents.
We've already shut down two copycats. You have? Yes. What did it cost you to do that?
I'm just curious. It cost us about $10,000. It was a smaller company. That's not bad, actually.
To us, that's bad. To us, it's bad. Yes. But it could have been a lot worse. How many more
have come back up? So right now, a very, very, very large handbag company came out with a handbag
raincoat. And on their website, they are calling it the handbag raincoat. What? No. And it costs
$650. Whoa. Okay. So guys, what do you need to start for? We're really looking for strategic
partners and growth capital. We have other ideas that... We have backpack raincoats for backpacks.
Shoe covers. We can expand on the brand to fully own the rain. This product is our blood,
sweat, and tears. Our parents have really supported us. We're willing to do whatever
it takes to make it work. No, you're not. Because we know it's going to work. I don't see that,
guys. I'm sorry. I'll be brutally honest. I don't see that you're willing to do everything it takes.
I get the vision, and I get the goal. But in the interim, you've had all these peaks where
you've had opportunity, and it hasn't really translated. You're hoping something triggers
so that the pull-through is there. And when you're hoping for that as opposed to controlling
your own destiny, that's why it's been challenging. And for those reasons, I'm out.
But I wish you guys the best. Thank you. Thank you.
I want to tell you the way I'm seeing it. You said you had no marketing or advertising,
which was a plus. Correct. I see that as a negative, because marketing and advertising
is going to be one of your most major expenses you're going to have to encounter if you're
going to grow this business. You said that you had a potential lawsuit. You spent $10,000 so far.
That's nothing compared to what you're going to have to do to stop a giant. I'm out.
Listen, I see so many similarities from my journey with you guys.
I was knocked off by a lot of big guys right when I came out. I had a patent on my product.
I chose to keep inventing and coming out with the next best product. But it's a personal decision.
I chose not to sue and not to go after everybody. Based on how much I would use the product myself,
which is part of what I like to invest in, I'm out. But I wish you guys great success.
Thank you. Thank you.
Okay. Let me give it to you by the numbers. Love numbers. This business is not worth $1.2 million.
You overvalued yourself by a factor of 10 times. I was intrigued by the fact that a behemoth
knocked you off, because I know how to fix those problems very quickly.
But- Help us solve that.
No, but I love to pour money on the gasoline of sales, not litigation. I know how to fix it,
but that's not a great place to build value. You're not going to stay on the shelves of retail
with 125,000 sales. Sorry to have to give you the bad news. The stereo stuff, all that, I love it.
So help us out. The numbers tell the truth. And honestly, I can't- We'll make you proud. We will.
There's just no way to make money with you. I'm out.
So I think the umbrella is why you haven't sold more. If you have an umbrella,
it's protecting everything, including the purse. It's cute. It's kitschy.
Lori, I'll pass the deal.
I feel so bad, because I adore both of you. I'd hire both of you. But on this product, I'm out.
Thank you. Thank you, guys. Congratulations on what you've accomplished.
Thank you. Can we just say to them, good luck in unison? One, two, three.
Good luck. Thank you. Thank you. See you guys. Bye.
Bye.
